EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms blew some large trees down between Red Rock and Picacho and also dropped large hail.  Thunderstorms winds also blew down some large trees, some as high as 40 feet, near Oracle. Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding between Red Rock and Arizona City closing 15 roads and washing out one road stranding 60 residents.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused roof damage to two buildings and several signs were blown over.
Roads were closed due to flooding near san Manuel and Mammoth.
Nogales wash overflowed its banks. Several vehicles had to be pulled out of the wash. Several minor injuries were reported
Thunderstorm winds blew the roof off of a market, damaged the roof of the First Baptist Church, and uprooted or snapped the limbs off of 5-8 trees.
Thunderstorm winds blew down several power poles in Safford and the nearby towns of Thatcher and Pima.
Nickel size hail (0.88 in.) reported by a spotter near the community of Christopher Creek.

Eleven hikers and tourists were drowned by a flash flood in a narrow slot canyon 5 miles southeast of Page.  A  severe thunderstorm 3 to 5 miles upstream produced very heavy rain causing a 10-30 foot wall of water to crash down Antelope Canyon.  In the area of the flood fatalities the depth and width of Antelope Canyon varies from about 20 feet across and 30 feet deep to points where it is 200 feet deep but only two (2) feet across.  It is a popular site for hiking and photography where access is via rope ladders controlled by Navajo Nation representatives.  The eleven who died and a tour guide  were warned not to enter the canyon because of the flood potential from an approaching thunderstorm.  The tour guide alone survived after being carried several miles downstream by the flood.
Thunderstorm winds blew a wall down and bent some metal piping.
Thunderstorm winds blew down several power lines.

A  large tree was blown down by strong thunderstorm winds and fell down on a car in the community of Christopher Creek. Inside the car one person was seriously injured and 2 small children received minor injuries.
One and a half (1.5) inch diameter hail reported in Mayer.
Power poles were knocked over by mudslides in the Wahweap area and street flooding was reported in Page.

Several reports of flooding around the Chinle area. First reports were received northwest of Chinle near the community of Rough Rock where Reservation Route 59 was impassable due to floodwaters. The last reports were received near Chinle where Reservation Route 27 was also flooded over.

Heavy rain from training thunderstorms caused a flash flood along Cataract Creek in the village of Supai, capital of the Havasupai Tribe. Power, water and sewage were washed out. Many homes and structures flooded. About 400 campers and residents were evacuated by helicopter. Red cross, BIA, National Guard, the Arizona DPS, a contract helicopter firm, and local law enforcement participated in the evacuation.

A large white water raft was washed away during minor flooding along Havasu Creek and the Colorado River in Grand Canyon National Park. No people were in the raft and it was recovered, but damaged, several days later.
One inch diameter hail reported at the Wupatki National Monument.
Minor rural road flooding in the Snowflake area.

Strong Thunderstorm wind caused some damage in the Chino Valley area.  Electrical power was knocked out when lightning hit a transformer.  The power outage lasted 30 minutes.  A cabana was torn off a trailer home.  A  section of the cabana with a swamp cooler attached was thrown 50 feet, while the rest of the cabana's roof was thrown 100 feet by the wind, and landed in an adjacent street.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down several telephone poles.
Steamboat Wash flooded over Indian Reservation Route 15 just west of Greasewood.

Three public reports were received of one inch diameter hail in the western sections of Flagstaff. Also in conjunction to this report a weather spotter reported minor street flooding in Flagstaff shortly after the hail.
Funnel cloud sighted by a spotter 1 mile west of the intersection of 61st Street and Dynamite Boulevard.
Shingles were blown off roofs and awnings were damaged at several homes.
A weather service employee near Broadway Road and Rural Road reported shingles ripped off his home. His neighbour reported a eucalyptus tree was sheared off at the top.
Centennial Wash filled with water due to heavy thunderstorm rains. La Paz County Sheriff's Office reported that the water flowed across Highway 60 near Wenden and caused some damage to the highway.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down six power poles.

An outbreak of thunderstorms in Scottsdale produced very heavy rainfall which caused some localized flooding. Automobiles were stuck in Indian Bend Wash at McCormick Parkway between Hayden Road and Scottsdale Road. Water flowing through Indian Bend Wash rose to three feet which forced some roads to close for a time. Rainfall totals around the area were between one and two inches.
Power lines were blown down across Interstate 10 near the Chandler Boulevard exit.
Between 48th Street and 52nd Street near Baseline Road, trees were blown down; at the South Pointe Resort, strong winds twisted metal beams and blew a roof partially off.
Funnel cloud spotted south-southwest of the Superstition Freeway near Dobson Road.
A rain-wrapped funnel cloud was sighted about 5 to 7 miles south of the intersection of Hardy Drive at University Drive.
In the area of Pima Road at McDonald Drive, reports of chaparral trees and fences down, roofs blown off, and plate glass windows blown out.
Wind gust recorded at Scottsdale Community College.
Damaging thunderstorm winds whipped through Kingman tearing half the roof from a motel and downing several trees and power poles.
Golf ball sized hail significantly damaged crops.
Wind knocked down several power lines.
Residental outbuildings flooded when a normally dry wash overflowed.  Up to a foot of water washed over several rural roads in low lying areas in and near Camp Verde.
Minor rural flooding over state road 96 between Hillside and Bagdad. Some rock slides were also reported near the road.
Lightning started a fire at a condominium complex.

Flash flooding was reported along Indian Reservation Route 60 just northwest of Dilkon.  Strong thunderstorms with heavy rain 5 miles northeast of Dilkon, produced heavy runoff in Dilkon.  Several washes and drainage ditches flooded sending up to 5 foot deep water through the Navajo Tribal Utilities Administration office and maintenance yard and flooding route 60.  About 100 feet of chain link fence was destroyed and several vehicles suffered water damage.  Twelve 30 foot long telephone poles that were stored in the maintenance yard were carried across the yard and into the fence.
Funnel Cloud sighted by off-duty NWS Meteorologist.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Radar estimated rainfall up to 6 inches caused flooding across road south of Topawa in which one vehicle was washed away.  Further west flood waters went across many spots along highway 85 near Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument and east of Ajo on highway 86.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Aravaipa Canyon Wilderness area southeast of Dudleyville caused a 4-5 foot rise in Aravaipa Creek.
Heavy rain from thunderstorms sent a one foot wall of water down a dry wash at Harrison Road and Pantano in far eastern Tucson preventing traffic from crossing.
Thunderstorm winds caused a dust storm near Picacho reducing visibility to near zero at times.
Thunderstorms dumped 2-3 inches of rain which caused five homes to be flooded. Damage was mainly to carpet and furniture.
Six illegal aliens attempting to cross into the United States from Mexico were drowned when they tried to cross a flooded drainage ditch.
Tornado captured on video tape touched down in open country for about 50 feet. No damage was observed.
Flooding was reported across state highway 191.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought heavy rain to Rio Rico causing flooding across several roads and also washed debris across Interstate 19.  One vehicle was stuck in the water.  winds blew down several street signs. This same thunderstorms caused flooding across Elephant Head Road near Sahuarita.

Thunderstorms brought heavy rain to Rio Rico causing flooding across several roads and also washed debris across Interstate 19.  One vehicle was stuck in the water.  winds blew down several street signs. This same thunderstorms caused flooding across Elephant Head Road near Sahuarita.

Thunderstorms brought heavy rain to Rio Rico causing flooding across several roads and also washed debris across Interstate 19.  One vehicle was stuck in the water.  winds blew down several street signs. This same thunderstorms caused flooding across Elephant Head Road near Sahuarita.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second of two microbursts struck Casa Blanca, this one with more fury. It produced damage in the vicinity of Southern Road and Ruins Road, and destroyed three trailers, snapped eight power poles, knocked down trees, and caused minor injuries to one individual.
Downed power lines on Dome Valley Road. A woman was trapped in her car due to the downed power lines. Further, 15 power poles were downed in Dome Valley.
Slow-moving thunderstorms pounded Dateland with a rainfall total of 3 inches. As a result, some side roads were barricaded due to high water.

Strong thunderstorm winds damaged a school, a storage building, and several porches and awnings. AC units and awnings at the school sustained $7,000 damage and residential areas sustained $20,000 total damage.
Thunderstorms produced damaging winds which knocked down some trees near Salome.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major winter storm brought heavy snow throughout northern Arizona.  In Zone 4 the Kaibab Plateau reported up to 24 inches of new snow and even Fredonia received 10 inches.  In Zone 6 the South Rim of the Grand Canyon received 12 inches.  In Zone 7 Seligman received 8 inches of new snow along with a report of 10 inches was received 5 miles north of Ash Fork.  In Zone 8 Jerome received 12 inches and even in Sedona 8 inches of new snow fell.  In Zone 9 Pinon reported as much as 12 inches of new snow.  In Zone 11 Window Rock reported 12 inches of new snow.  In Zone 15 Bellemont reported 19.5 inches of and Williams received 12 inches.  In Zone 16 Happy Jack reported 24 inches of new snow and Munds Park just south of Flagstaff received 19 inches of new snow.  In Zone 17 the largest storm total was reported 5 miles southeast of Pinetop where 30 inches of new snow fell.  Elsewhere in the zone, Pinetop received 16 inches of new snow, McNary 14 inches, Alpine 13 inches and Show Low had 12 inches.  Finally in Zone 18 Strawberry and Payson both reported 12 inches of new snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of state highway 99 south of Leupp.  Two drainage ditches and a small stock pond filled rapidly with water which then flowed across highway 99, 2 miles south of Leupp. Arizona Dept. of Transportation road crews reported one to two feet of water flowing rapidly across a 100 foot stretch of the highway and eroding embankments on either side of the road.
Several roads and wash crossings closed due to flooding. Two and one half feet of water flowing in Brawley wash at Three Points.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Up to 12 inches of snow fell on the Northwest Arizona plateau and deserts above 4000 feet during a 24 hour period.  Chloride reported a foot of snow on the ground and 15 to 18 inches fell on Hualapai Mountain above 6000 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several power lines, blew a stoplight down, snapped a 30-foot Palm tree in half,  and split a 15-foot high tree in two.
Lightning struck a house and started a fire.
Thunderstorm winds were estimated to 60 mph.
Minor street flooding in the Flagstaff area.
Minor road flooding over state route 277 near Heber.

In the vicinity of Casa Blanca Road and Ruins Road, a microburst caused damage to a softball field where dugouts were blown over and smashed, with one piece of dugout blown across the road. It also blew a prefabricated home off its foundation. A satellite dish was uprooted, and two power poles snapped. Roof damage was observed, a stop sign was twisted and blown over, and an awning was blown off at the southeast corner of Casa Blanca Road and Ruins Road.
Several road closures due to flooding.
The Mia Casa mobile home park in the west portion of Nogales was flooded with water 3-4 feet deep. Damage to a few cars and several homes.
In west Tucson one car was caught in flooded wash with water above the windows. Also several underpasses were flooded with 1-2 feet of water.
Thunderstorm winds gusting to 78 kts snapped a power pole knocking out power to 1700 customers and toppled a tree onto a house.
A 72 year old man suffered burns on his right leg and pelvis when lightning struck nearby.

Downburst winds from nearby thunderstorms kicked up a thick cloud of dust as it moved across plowed fields.  This cloud of dust then moved across interstate 10 between Red Rock and Picacho reducing visibilities to zero at times.  This resulted in 12 collisions involving about 30 vehicles.  Twenty-five people were injured, but 19 were only minor.
Dirt roads in the area were impassable due to flooding.
Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees and power lines. Shingles, tar paper and sheets of tin were blown off buildings and roofs.
The Quartzsite Marshal's Office reported quarter-sized hail in the east side of town.
Strong winds snapped several APS power poles near Neely Street and Pinon Way. Wind gust estimated from damage.
Heavy thunderstorm rains produced running water over roads along Highway 72 northwest of Bouse. The flowing water carried rocks, tree stumps, and railroad ties across the roads.

Lightning struck a house in Chandler slightly injuring a woman. It struck phone lines, power lines, and plumbing in the house igniting a blaze which caused an estimated $50,000 damage to the upstairs bathroom, bedrooms, and attic. The woman was injured while talking on the phone when the lightning travelled along the phone cord and grazed her face and neck.

A weather spotter at McKellips and Mesa Drive reported a ham radio tower had blown down and his neighbours' television antennas had either blown down or buckled from the strong winds. He also reported blown-down fences in his neighbourhood.
Strong winds knocked down fifteen power line poles on Arizona 287 between Florence and Coolidge.
Reports of several downed power poles.
Several roads were flooded in Cornville. Two cars were totally submerged in a wash.  The vehicle owners parked the cars in the wash when it was dry to go off-road on motorcycles.
Minor flooding at the Castle Canyon Mesa area in Prescott Valley.
0.75 inch diameter hail reported by the public.
Firefighters pulled three people from their cars when they became stranded in swift 2-foot-deep water in Pantano Wash at South Harrison Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms blew some large trees down between Red Rock and Picacho and also dropped large hail.  Thunderstorms winds also blew down some large trees, some as high as 40 feet, near Oracle. Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding between Red Rock and Arizona City closing 15 roads and washing out one road stranding 60 residents.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Benson police reported flooding in downtown Benson and numerous traffic accidents on Interstate 10 due to zero visibility in heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms blew some large trees down between Red Rock and Picacho and also dropped large hail.  Thunderstorms winds also blew down some large trees, some as high as 40 feet, near Oracle. Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding between Red Rock and Arizona City closing 15 roads and washing out one road stranding 60 residents.

Thunderstorms blew some large trees down between Red Rock and Picacho and also dropped large hail.  Thunderstorms winds also blew down some large trees, some as high as 40 feet, near Oracle. Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding between Red Rock and Arizona City closing 15 roads and washing out one road stranding 60 residents.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 55 to 60 mph caused minor roof damage to several buildings.
A weather spotter in Kingman observed one inch diameter hail.
Minor flooding of dirt roads and ponding of water in Winona.
Strong thunderstorm winds downed several telephone poles in Strawberry.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked out phone service.
Three quarter inch diameter hail reported by the public and covering the ground to 7 inches deep.  Dense fog developed as the hail melted reducing visibility to less than 1/4 mile.
Thunderstorm winds blew down several power lines.
Minor flooding in the Heber area.
Minor flooding in the Show Low area.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick snowstorm hit the White Mountains.  In Zone 17 twelve inches fell in Alpine and ten inches fell in Sunrise Ski Resort and Greer.  In Zone 14 nine inches fell in Springerville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Single thunderstorm gust to 59 kts/68 mph near Beaver Creek Country Club in Lake Montezuma.
Spotter reported a funnel cloud but the report was unconfirmed and only minor thunderstorms were in the area at the time.

A  thunderstorm caused wind damage to eastern sections of Camp Verde. One report was received from the public of a tornado, but no witnesses could be found to verify this.  We concluded the damage was caused by gusty winds, but no winds speeds were recorded. Several trailers were moved off their foundations in the White Hills Trailer Park.  On Hardy Lane branches broke off trees and were scattered over the road. In the Sierra Verde area an older barn lost part of its roof.
A severe thunderstorm that moved through Safford knocked down 6 power poles, blew part of a roof off a house ,  broke off several large tree limbs,  and damaged several other structures.
A severe thunderstorm dropped up to dime size hail turning the ground white.

Strong gradient winds resulted in numerous reports of sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph and a peak gust at Laughlin-Bullhead Airport of 58 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman camping with her two sons in a tent was killed when lightning struck the tent.  Her two sons, one inside the tent, the other outside the tent, were not injured.
A man camping in his tent was killed when lightning struck his tent.
A tin roof was blown off a shed.

A weather spotter in Lake Havasu City reported a 69 mph wind gust from a nearby thunderstorm.  There was no report of damage.

A cold winter storm created snowfall at unusually low elevations. A trace of snow was recorded at Tucson, and 4 to 10 inches at elevations between 4000 and 6000 feet.  This storm closed schools, stranded many motorists, caused broken water pipes, and caused the death of many ostriches at commercial farms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 55kts.
Road was closed due to boulder being washed onto road. Water overflowed banks of wash.
Dime size hail was reported.

A dust devil caused damage to four homes, toppled a block wall, ripped a roof off of a shed, and knocked down a few large trees. The damage occurred near the intersection of Paradise Lane and 53rd Avenue.
Wind gust was estimated by a weather spotter at Apache Trail and Ellsworth Road.

High winds, associated with the January 06-07 winter storm, were reported in Pine, in Northwestern Gila County. A peak gust of 62 MPH/54 Knots was recorded at 6:53 PM MST.  Law enforcement reported many trees knocked over causing power and telephone outages.  Trees blocked a dozen roads and fell on 2 houses causing minor damage.  A tree fell on one car causing significant damage.  Similiarly, in the Arizona Snowbowl ski area, just north of Flagstaff, high winds occurred around 9:00 PM causing trees to fall on powerlines. No wind speed was recorded, but the winds were estimated in excess of 50 knots.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust was estimated by a spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short-lived tornado, gust front related, observed by the public 8 miles north of Valle.  Shifted two mobile homes on their foundation.  A third mobile home was pushed off its foundation.
Short-lived tornado crossed Highway 64 near Valle.  Observed by two Sheriff's officers and only lasted 5-10 seconds.  No known damage.

A major winter storm brought heavy snow mainly along the Mogollon Rim and the White Mountains. In the Western Mogollon Rim (AZZ015)  Doney Park (eastern Flagstaff) received 7 inches of snow with some blowing snow and the Arizona Snowbowl ski area (7 N ofFlagstaff) received 14 inches of new snow at 10,800 feet. In the Eastern Mogollon rim (AZZ016) Happy Jack received 8 inches of new snow.  In the White Mountains (AZZ018) Alpine received 14 inches and the Sunrise ski area on Baldy Peak reported 24 inches of new snow.  This storm brought some high winds with areas of blowing and drifting snow which closed some state roads in the Mogollon Rim i southern Coconino and Navajo Counties.

A quick storm dropped heavy wet snow overnight on the Kaibab Plateau.  The deepest snowfall amount was reported 18 miles south of Jacob Lake where 16 inches of snow fell at about 8500 feet MSL.

This storm brought some high winds with areas of blowing and drifting snow which closed some state roads in the Mogollon Rim i southern Coconino and Navajo Counties.

A cold winter storm created snowfall at unusually low elevations. A trace of snow was recorded at Tucson, and 4 to 10 inches at elevations between 4000 and 6000 feet.  This storm closed schools, stranded many motorists, caused broken water pipes, and caused the death of many ostriches at commercial farms.

Perhaps the largest snow storm of the decade brought heavy snow to most of northern Arizona. Heavy snow fell from early Sunday morning, the 12th, through last Tuesday night, the 14th. Four deaths from exposure occurred during, or immediately after the storm, on the Navajo Nation Reservation and were directly related to this catastropic winter storm.  The Governor of Arizona declared a State of Emergency for Coconino, Gila and Navajo counties on Tuesday the 14th. Following the storm, National Guard trucks and helicopters were needed to evacuate people on the Navajo Nation who required medical attention due to chronic medical problems and who were unable to obtain needed medication. National Guard helicopters also dropped food to people and livestock who were stranded for several days following the storm. Unofficial snow accumulations up to 6 feet were reported along the Mogollon Rim in extreme southeastern Coconino county. Very strong winds created drifts as high as 10 feet at many wind-prone areas across northern Arizona.  Numerous trees fell on cars, houses and roads causing power outages and property damage.  Hundreds of miles of major highways were closed mainly along the Mogollon Rim and the White Mountains area. Highways surrounding Flagstaff were the most affected. Interstate 40 from Winslow to Ashfork and Interstate 17 from Cordes Junction to Flagstaff were closed from noon Monday, Jan. 13 through 6 AM MST, Wednesday, Jan. 15. Over 200 vehicles were stranded on these two highways.The heavy snow in Flagstaff caused Northern Arizona University to close for the first time in 20 years. Flagstaff public schools were closed for five days. This was the 12th biggest snow storm in Flagstaff's 100 years of weather records.     In the Western Mogollon Rim (AZZ015): unofficial snowfall of 38 inches was reported in Flagstaff.  Official amounts included 30.8 inches at Bellemont, 26 inches in Doney Park with 5 foot drifts, 25 inches in Sunset Crater and 28 inches at Walnut Canyon. A higher elevations, Hart Prairie received 42 inches and the local ski area reported 51 inches of new snow. Winds as high as 50 MPH caused considerable blowing and drifting snow.In the Eastern Mogollon Rim (AZZ016): between 5 to 6 feet of snow fell according to unofficial reports from Forest Lakes Estates. Official reports included 52 inches at Happy Jack and Lake Mary reported 33 inches of snow. Snow accumulations of 52 inches were reported at Heber and Overgaard, with an unofficial report from the public of 5 feet of snow. In Northern Gila County (AZZ018): the Christopher Creek area in NW Gila county reported 4 feet of snow.  Lower elevation stations such as Strawberry reported 2 1/2 feet of snow.  Many trees fell due to strong winds and heavy wet snow causing property damage and power outages that lasted 72 hours.In the White Mountains (AZZ017): Greer reported 28 inches of snow while Show Low reported 18 inches. Pinetop received 24 inches with 18 inches accumulating in Lakeside.In the Chuska Mountains/Defiance Plateau (AZZ011): Window Rock reported 18 inches of snow with 5 foot drifts. There were unofficial reports of up to 36 inches on snow in the Black Mesa areas on the Navajo Nation (northern Navajo and Apache counties). A 63 year old man died of exposure 10 miles SE of Chinle.In the Chinle Valley (AZZ010): up to 3 feet of snow was reported unofficially in the higher mesas. One 37 year old male died of exposure near Many Farms.In the Northeast Mesas and Plateaus (AZZ009): unofficial reports of up to 3 feet of snow fell on the higher mesas. A 50 year old man died of exposure 3/4 mile west of Steamboat. A 64 year old infirm man died in his home in Indian Wells when he was unable to start a fire in his wood stove.In the Grand Canyon Country (AZZ006): one foot of snow was reported at the South Rim of the Grand Canyon. While in the Kaibab Plateau (AZZ004) there were unofficial reports of up to two feet of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Srong winds covered much of southeast. Arizona. Winds overturned half of a mobile home being transported on Interstate 10 near Benson. Winds of 40 to 50 mph were common with areas of blowing dust. Strongest winds were near Fort Huachuca where the peak wind of 62 kts was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds, associated with the January 06-07 winter storm, were reported in Pine, in Northwestern Gila County. A peak gust of 62 MPH/54 Knots was recorded at 6:53 PM MST.  Law enforcement reported many trees knocked over causing power and telephone outages.  Trees blocked a dozen roads and fell on 2 houses causing minor damage.  A tree fell on one car causing significant damage.  Similiarly, in the Arizona Snowbowl ski area, just north of Flagstaff, high winds occurred around 9:00 PM causing trees to fall on powerlines. No wind speed was recorded, but the winds were estimated in excess of 50 knots.

Over 200 vehicles were stranded on these two highways.The heavy snow in Flagstaff caused Northern Arizona University to close for the first time in 20 years. Flagstaff public schools were closed for five days. This was the 12th biggest snow storm in Flagstaff's 100 years of weather records.     In the Western Mogollon Rim (AZZ015): unofficial snowfall of 38 inches was reported in Flagstaff.  Official amounts included 30.8 inches at Bellemont, 26 inches in Doney Park with 5 foot drifts, 25 inches in Sunset Crater and 28 inches at Walnut Canyon. A higher elevations, Hart Prairie received 42 inches and the local ski area reported 51 inches of new snow. Winds as high as 50 MPH caused considerable blowing and drifting snow.In the Eastern Mogollon Rim (AZZ016): between 5 to 6 feet of snow fell according to unofficial reports from Forest Lakes Estates. Official reports included 52 inches at Happy Jack and Lake Mary reported 33 inches of snow. Snow accumulations of 52 inches were reported at Heber and Overgaard, with an unofficial report from the public of 5 feet of snow. In Northern Gila County (AZZ018): the Christopher Creek area in NW Gila county reported 4 feet of snow.  Lower elevation stations such as Strawberry reported 2 1/2 feet of snow.  Many trees fell due to strong winds and heavy wet snow causing property damage and power outages that lasted 72 hours.In the White Mountains (AZZ017): Greer reported 28 inches of snow while Show Low reported 18 inches. Pinetop received 24 inches with 18 inches accumulating in Lakeside.In the Chuska Mountains/Defiance Plateau (AZZ011): Window Rock reported 18 inches of snow with 5 foot drifts. There were unofficial reports of up to 36 inches on snow in the Black Mesa areas on the Navajo Nation (northern Navajo and Apache counties). A 63 year old man died of exposure 10 miles SE of Chinle.In the Chinle Valley (AZZ010): up to 3 feet of snow was reported unofficially in the higher mesas. One 37 year old male died of exposure near Many Farms.In the Northeast Mesas and Plateaus (AZZ009): unofficial reports of up to 3 feet of snow fell on the higher mesas. A 50 year old man died of exposure 3/4 mile west of Steamboat. A 64 year old infirm man died in his home in Indian Wells when he was unable to start a fire in his wood stove.In the Grand Canyon Country (AZZ006): one foot of snow was reported at the South Rim of the Grand Canyon. While in the Kaibab Plateau (AZZ004) there were unofficial reports of up to two feet of snow.

A major early spring storm struck the mountains of central Arizona mainly around W. Flagstaff.  Most of the snow fell by mid afternoon on April 2nd.  Snowfall totals at that time included 25 inches at Bellemont (11 miles west of Flagstaff), 18 inches in N. Flagstaff, 16 inches in W. Flagstaff and 8 inches at Munds Park (13 miles south of Flagstaff).  The 28.0 inches that fell in Bellemont on April 2nd set the daily record snowfall for that day and for the entire month of April.  Snow continued to fall for the next 3 days making the total snowfall for all 4 days at Bellemont with 44.3 inches.

A powerful thunderstorm unleashed a destructive wind that wreaked havoc on a small section of Bullhead City, AZ.  The fierce wind snapped 24 power poles and damaged approximately 80 houses and mobile homes.  Roofs were blown off or damaged, a garage was blown away and numerous awnings were ripped off and hurled through the air.  22 of the homes were condemned.
La Cholla Road at Canada Del Oro Wash was closed due to water flowing over the road.
Some street flooding reported in East-Central Tucson.
Thunderstorms producing heavy rain caused some street flooding.
About 30 power poles downed near Patterson Road.
Widespread street and low land flooding.
Deer Valley airport reported a peak gust of 92mph.
Heavy rainfall left many low spots and highway dips flooded between Bouse and Parker.
Spotters and law enforcement reported power lines and trees down.
A Pacific storm that moved across southern California and southern Arizona left between 18 and 24 inches of snow in the Hualapai Mountains of Mohave County during an 18 hour period.
Heavy thunderstorm rains flooded several roads in Camp Verde, AZ causing over a dozen traffic accidents.
Highway 260 in Cottonwood flooded by heavy thunderstorm rains.
Thunderstorm wind gust of 56 kts/64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms over the southern and western portion of the county produced up to 13/4 inch hail and flooding that stranded several vehicles in washes and caused road closures.

An estimated $150 to $200 million in damage was sustained by crops throughout Yuma County due mainly to flooded crops. About $30 to $40 million was to lemon trees. The heavy rain was attributed to Tropical Storm Nora.
Dime-sized hail and winds up to 51 mph were observed by a storm spotter.
Spotters reported trellises and shingles damaged in North Phoenix.
Numerous power outages.
NWS employee estimated wind gusts to 60 mph.
Heavy rain, associated with Tropical Storm Nora, produced widespread street flooding in Yuma. Between 1.5 and 3.0 inches of rain fell from around 10 am until around 2 pm.
Heavy rain, with total rainfall of up to 3 inches, fell in Parker. The rain was associated with Tropical Storm Nora. There was a foot of standing water on many roads and many more were closed.

Three mobile homes were washed out, one of which had a family inside. There were no reports of deaths or injuries. All were rescued and placed in an emergency shelter. The rain was due to Tropical Storm Nora.
Widespread flooding occurred in the town of Aguila. Many properties and roads were under six inches of water. There was also a report of water flowing across Eagle Eye Road in western Aguila.
Dime-sized hail was reported by an NWS employee at Arizona Avenue and Pecos Road.
Tropical Storm Nora produced upwards of 3 inches of rainfall in Somerton. Water got to about 3 feet deep, forcing the evacuation of about 750 residents from two mobile home parks.

Strong winds from Tropical Storm Nora produced some damage in the Yuma area. There were reports of shingles missing from roofs, awnings torn off, street signs blown overpower lines were downed, and scattered power outages. Shortly after 2 pm MST, winds gusted briefly to 54 mph in Yuma.
One building was blown down and another had half of its roof blown off.  Several power lines wereknocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several power lines and trees in the east part of Tucson.  A 6 foot brick wall was damaged by a falling tree.  The winds blew the roof off of one home.  A woman was injured when she was struck by lightning.  Heavy rains from these thunderstorms caused flooding of several streets in east Tucson with several vehicles trapped in the waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mohave County Sheriff's Office reported sustained winds at an estimated 60 mph, with gusts up to 75 mph, over an area extending from the Mohave Valley to Lake Havasu City, AZ.  Visibilities were reduced to zero and some homes in Lake Havasu City sustained minor damage to roofs and windows.
The Bullhead City Fire Department reported damaging winds resulting in broken tree branches, downed power lines, awnings torn off mobile homes and a blown down shed.

Strong winds blowing down the Colorado River Valley produced light damage in Bullhead City, AZ.  Although wind gusts of only 48 mph were recorded at the Laughlin-Bullhead Airport, several awnings were broken or ripped from homes and a few sheds were damaged.
Off-duty NWS employee reported the touchdown of a weak tornado.
National Parks personnel reported a funnel cloud over the entrance of the Petrified Forest Wilderness.

Heavy rains from these thunderstorms caused flooding of several streets in east Tucson with several vehicles trapped in the waters.

Heavy rain produced flooding at Katherine's Landing, a marina on Lake Mohave.
Trees blown over in the Yuma foothills. Lattices were blown down and awnings were damaged. Wind speed was estimated.
Power poles were knocked down. Wind speed was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms raked Lake Havasu City between 5:50 and 6:25 PM MST.  Wind from the storms reportedly ripped trees out of the ground, downed power lines, and tore tiles from roofs.  The city was also pelted with heavy rain and 3/4 inch diameter hail on the south side of town and up to one inch diameter hail on the north side.  Subsequent flash flooding of washes swept away several vehicles and required swift water rescues of the occupants.  No serious injuries were reported.

Roofs were blown off garages, and tin sheds were blown around.
Winds blew down several large trees which subsequently knocked down power lines.
Portion of a roof blew off a house in east Mesa.
Several more power lines blown down in Tempe.

Thunderstorms dumped between 2-3 inches of rain over the western portion of Nogales causing flash flooding that damaged about 100 homes and 30 businesses.  The flooding forced the evacuation of 150 residents from their homes.  The floodwaters carried 5 vehicles down Western Avenue, the most heavily damaged street.
Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flooding of washes.
Thunderstorms dumping up to 1 inch of rain caused some flooding of roadways in northwest Tucson.
Clay tiles blown off roof of Target store.
Several power lines blown down in northwest Phoenix.
Several large trees down in east Mesa.
Power lines blown down.
Power lines blown down.
Three funnel clouds were sighted.
Large two-foot diameter tree uprooted at the intersection of Longmore Road and Southern Avenue.
Part of a trailer roof was blown off. Signs along a road were blown down.

Three to five inches of rain which fell from Nora led to some flash flooding in portions of northwest Maricopa County. Two earthen dams gave way in Aguila and caused widespread flooding. One dike was located seven miles east of Aguila and the second in the center of the Martori Farms complex. Half of the cotton crop was lost at Martori Farms, as well as 300 to 500 acres of melons. Up to five feet of water filled Aguila. About 40 people were evacuated from the hardest hit area of the town.	Water flowing down the Sols Wash was so high that the Sols Wash Bridge in Wickenburg was closed for more than two hours. There was some flooding below Sols Wash in the streets around Coffinger Park. Several houses in the area were flooded. Highway 71 west of Wickenburg and Highway 95 north were closed due to high water from the storm.
The public reported that two funnel clouds occurred simultaneously, about 3 miles apart, over White Mountain Lake.

The Canyon Lake Marina was relocated 50 feet due to thunderstorm winds estimated near 80 mph. Numerous boats, 360 in all, shifted 50 feet east or north. During the strong winds, the relocated marina and several of the boats sustained damage.
Several downed power poles.
A storm spotter reported that washes near Truxton were filling with water after an hour of heavy rain.
A storm spotter in Truxton reported that strong winds from a nearby thunderstorm knocked over a fence and broke small tree limbs.
Nickel-sized hail was observed in Truxton by a storm spotter.

Damaging winds ripped part of a roof off a house, downed several trees and knocked over a fence. Although the exact time was not known, a Skywarn Spotter also recorded a 75 mph thunderstorm gust that occurred sometime between 5:30 and 8:00 pm MST.  Other events that occurred between 6 and 8 pm MST included power outages, a roof blown off a fitness center, apartment parking awnings mangled and street signs blown over and bent.
A Skywarn Spotter reported golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with very heavy rain began over central Mohave County around 12:30 am and ended around 2:30 am MST.  Washes rapidly filled in the vicinity of Kingman and several roads were washed out.  At least two cars were caught in a flooded wash and their four occupants had to be rescued by helicopter.  Also, one woman was found dead hours later in a sewer drainage pond.  It is unknown how she was caught in the  flood waters.  Another serious result occurred a few hours after the storms ended when a passenger train derailed while crossing a small bridge damaged and weakened by flood waters.  Of the 302 passengers and crew members aboard, 116 were injured and of those eight sustained serious injuries.

Truxton wash was filling rapidly and considerable water was running in Yampai and Peach Springs Canyons.  The Forest Service estimated that up to three inches of rain had fallen.
Heavy thunderstorm rains caused mudslides and flooding in the Wahweep area of Page, AZ closing Lakeshore Drive.
Nickel-sized hail (0.88 inch diameter) reported at Walnut Canyon National Monument.

Heavy rain from several thunderstorms caused widespread street flooding in south and west Flagstaff.  Several traffic accidents were caused by heavy rain, poor visibility, and water on Interstates 40 and 17 in south Flagstaff.  In west Flagstaff the westbound lane of Route 66 was closed due to ponding water.
Dense fog reported 6 miles west of Benson on interstate 10.

Heavy rain and flooding was reported in Golden Valley, just west of Kingman.  The same storm spotter also reported 1.6 inches of rain in less than an hour and washes running full on the east side of Kingman.

Widespread heavy rain from Tropical Storm Nora spread across Yavapai County on 25th through the morning of the 26th.  Flooding, flash flooding, and urban flooding occurred in and around Bagdad, Prescott, Yarnell, Peeples Valley, north of Wickenburg (Maricopa County), and Aguila.  Flooding and rock/mud slides closed several state and county roads, and city streets for several hours both days in and around the cities mentioned. Crown King...............	4.79 in.				Prescott Airport........	2.37 in.
Thunderstorm winds damaged the roof of the high school and  trees were uprooted throughout town.

Hail covered the ground up to six inches deep. An NWS employee had roof tile broken and glass window broken on his house.Damage was sustained by aircraft at Falcon Field. Apache Wells RV/Golf Resort had damage to TV antennas, and damage to roofs and awnings. Citrus trees lost many of their leaves and fruit in the vicinity of Greenfield and Brown Roads. Some other trees were stripped of half or more of their leaves.
Large to moderate-sized tree limbs were snapped by thunderstorm winds at Falcon Field. Metal awnings were damaged by the wind at Apache Wells RV/Golf Resort.
Flooding occurred across Highway 188 south of Punkin Center. A foot of mud debris moved onto the highway.
Sand bags were used on Main Street due to one to two feet of water flowing across the road. Weather spotter recorded 2.5 inches of rain from the storm.
A Skywarn Spotter reported dime-sized hail which caused minor damage to his house.
A Skywarn Spotter reported several roads around Kingman eroded and damaged and one road completely washed out.  Many locations around the city had up to a foot of standing water.
The runoff from several thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flooding on several streets in Payson.
One adult male was seriously injured when struck by lightning.

Heavy rain caused several minor roads to flood and become impassible while ponding water near the Little Colorado River flooded one house.  Water up to one foot deep entered the house, which suffered minor damage to the first floor.

Downburst winds from nearby thunderstorm knocked a mobile home off its foundation completely demolishing it.  The winds also blew a cooler off the roof of another trailer, blew over a couple of sheds and torn some of the singles off the roofs of other trailers.
Flash Flooding of Bright Angel Creek caused minor flooding in the Bright Angel Campground.
Several thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flooding on several secondary roads and one lane of  eastbound Interstate 40 through Winona was closed due to flooding for one hour.
Thunderstorm wind gust to 77 knots (88 mph).

A Flash Flood on Phantom Creek killed two people and injured a third person.  Runoff from the heavy rain associated with a thunderstorm several miles north of the flash flood site caught the three people as they were crossing Phantom Creek.  All three attempted to save themselves by crouching behind a large boulder, but chest-deep water carried them downstream.  The bodies of the two who were killed were found several weeks later, miles downstream in the Colorado River.  The survivor was carried one quarter mile down Phantom Creek then another quarter mile down Bright Angel Creek before he was able to escape and hike out of the canyon.
National Weather Service employee witnessed a short-lived and weak tornado.

Two German visitors to Grand Canyon National Park were struck by lightning as they observed a thunderstorm from an isolated point overlooking the Grand Canyon.  The adult male was seriously injured.  The adult female received minor injuries.
